Name: adaptive_rate_distortion_compression
Title: Adaptive Rate-Distortion Compression: Leveraging Data Distribution for Efficient Model Pruning
Short Hypothesis: Adaptive model compression strategies that account for the structure of the data distribution can achieve lower distortion rates compared to traditional methods that do not incorporate such information.
Related Work: Existing work on model compression primarily addresses the rate-distortion trade-off without fully leveraging the information provided by the data distribution. Papers like Isik et al. (2021) focus on pruning strategies but often overlook how the intrinsic data structure can inform more efficient compression strategies. This proposal distinguishes itself by explicitly integrating data distribution characteristics into the compression framework.
Abstract: This proposal aims to investigate adaptive model compression strategies that utilize information about the data distribution to optimize rate-distortion trade-offs. Traditional approaches to model compression, such as pruning and quantization, often rely on heuristics that do not consider the underlying structure of the data. By leveraging insights from rate-distortion theory, we propose a novel adaptive compression framework that adjusts the compression strategy based on the entropy and mutual information of the data distribution. This approach is expected to yield a more efficient representation, reducing task-relevant distortion while adhering to the operational bounds of information theory. We will evaluate our method against established benchmarks and existing compression techniques in terms of both distortion and predictive performance, providing theoretical insights into the benefits of incorporating data distribution information into model compression.
Experiments: 1. Implement a baseline model using standard pruning and quantization techniques to establish a performance baseline.
2. Develop an adaptive model compression algorithm that integrates data distribution information, such as entropy and mutual information estimates, into the pruning and quantization process.

3. Conduct experiments on benchmark datasets (e.g., CIFAR-10, ImageNet) to compare the performance of the adaptive approach against the baseline in terms of distortion metrics and predictive accuracy.
4. Analyze the trade-off curves of rate versus distortion for both approaches, highlighting the gains achieved by the adaptive method.
Risk Factors and Limitations: Potential risks include the accuracy of the data distribution estimates and the computational overhead of adaptive mechanisms. Limitations may arise in scenarios where data distributions do not significantly inform compression decisions or when the underlying model architecture is not amenable to adaptive strategies.
